title: φ:1:φ⁻¹ neural coordination allocation
summary: Appendix A's second mathematical claim: optimal conscious coordination across three bodies maintains the golden-ratio allocation |A|:|B|:|C| ≈ φ:1:φ⁻¹ (1.618:1:0.618). For neural systems: sensing gets φ, integration gets 1, action gets φ⁻¹. Honest caveat: unlike the dynamic claim in #01, this static allocation is not genuinely identifiable — it's vulnerable to confirmation bias (any 3-element system with roughly 1.6:1:0.6 proportions will read as 'approximately φ') and the appendix's Gurdjieff footnote retroactively reframes sacred geometry as mathematics. Treat this diagram as a hypothesis-generating frame, not a measurable claim.

nodes.label: Sensing Allocation
nodes.description: A — sensory input processing, proportion φ ≈ 1.618

nodes.label: Action Output
nodes.description: C — motor output and action, proportion φ⁻¹ ≈ 0.618

nodes.label: Golden Ratio Coherence
nodes.description: Optimal consciousness — claimed to emerge when A:B:C ≈ φ:1:φ⁻¹

edges.description: Sensing at φ proportion claimed to contribute to coherence
Integration at unit proportion claimed to contribute to coherence
Action at φ⁻¹ proportion claimed to contribute to coherence

identifiability_note: Downgraded from 'identifiable' on honest audit. Problems: (1) no peer-reviewed measurement of φ:1:φ⁻¹ in actual neural systems — the appendix cites no study; (2) confirmation bias is structural — any three-element system with proportions between 1.4 and 1.8 reads as 'approximately φ'; (3) the Gurdjieff footnote retroactively reframes numerology as mathematics, which is a post-hoc rationalization pattern. Unlike the dynamic S/E → π/φ claim in #01, nothing would falsify this allocation claim.
